coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of visible light responsive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/MIL-101(Cr) composites for enhanced photodegradation of Rhodamine B
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Rahmani, Milad
Abbasi, Alireza
Hosseini, Mahdiyeh-Sadat
coredata.dc:description: In this study, an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process was successfully utilized for the fabrication of MIL-101(Cr) (MIL-Cr) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) without employing toxic and hazardous hydrofluoric acid and organic solvents. Then, a simple solvent-deposition technique was used to prepare MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) composites without requiring specialized equipment. To assess the photocatalytic capabilities of the synthesized materials, Rhodamine B (RhB) was chosen as one of the wastewater toxic organic dye pollutants for photo-degradation experiments. The optimum amount of N-CQDs in composite structure was determined by mixing different volumes of N-CQDs with MIL-Cr, in which MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance (95%) under visible light illumination. Also, all composites showed better photocatalytic activity in comparison with pristine MOF and N-CQDs individually because N-CQDs can operate as electron acceptors when coupled with MOF, which can prevent the fast electron-hole recombination and increase the visible light absorption. Based on the results of the kinetic investigation, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) depicted the highest kinetic rate, which is 2.63 and 9.5 times larger than pristine MIL-Cr and N-CQDs under the same experimental conditions. Furthermore, a photocatalytic mechanism was postulated through reactive species scavenging experiments, and it was found that •OH and photo-generated h+ were crucial to the degradation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-101(Cr)
Carbon quantum dot
Composite
Photocatalyst
RhB degradation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Exploring the stability of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from University of Tehran .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XRD patterns of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.
N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms for MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.
EDX spectrum and elemental mapping of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.
FE-SEM images of (a) MIL-Cr and (b) MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite; (c) TEM and (d) HR-TEM images of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite (the inset picture shows the DLS graph of pure N-CQDs).
(a) DRS spectra, and (b) Tauc plots of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.
(a) M−S plot of MIL-Cr, (b) EIS Nyquist plots, and (c) ECSA of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.

Photocatalytic decomposition of RhB over (a) MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) composites, and (b) other photocatalytic systems, (c) effect of different dosage of H2O2, and (d) spectra of UV–Vis absorption for the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system. Reaction condition: RhB aqueous solution (50 mL, 20 ppm), H2O2 dosage (20 mM) and composite amount (20 mg).
(a) pseudo-first-order, and (b) pseudo-second-order diagrams of various photocatalytic systems.

(a) Effect of various scavengers on RhB degradation and (b) the reusability experiments of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2). Reaction condition: RhB aqueous solution (50 mL, 20 ppm), H2O2 dosage (10 mM) and composite amount (20 mg).
The possible mechanism for RhB degradation over MIL-Cr/N-CQDs.
RhB photodegradation kinetics constant of the different systems.
Comparison of other MOF-based photocatalysts for photodegradation of RhB with MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of visible light responsive nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/MIL-101(Cr) composites for enhanced photodegradation of Rhodamine B

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, P.O. Box: 14155-6455, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Tehran, P.O. Box: 14155-6455, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, an environmentally friendly hydrothermal process was successfully utilized for the fabrication of MIL-101(Cr) (MIL-Cr) and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) without employing toxic and hazardous hydrofluoric acid and organic solvents. Then, a simple solvent-deposition technique was used to prepare MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) composites without requiring specialized equipment. To assess the photocatalytic capabilities of the synthesized materials, Rhodamine B (RhB) was chosen as one of the wastewater toxic organic dye pollutants for photo-degradation experiments. The optimum amount of N-CQDs in composite structure was determined by mixing different volumes of N-CQDs with MIL-Cr, in which MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance (95%) under visible light illumination. Also, all composites showed better photocatalytic activity in comparison with pristine MOF and N-CQDs individually because N-CQDs can operate as electron acceptors when coupled with MOF, which can prevent the fast electron-hole recombination and increase the visible light absorption. Based on the results of the kinetic investigation, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) depicted the highest kinetic rate, which is 2.63 and 9.5 times larger than pristine MIL-Cr and N-CQDs under the same experimental conditions. Furthermore, a photocatalytic mechanism was postulated through reactive species scavenging experiments, and it was found that •OH and photo-generated h+ were crucial to the degradation process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Although modern industry has grown dramatically since the 20th century, pollution of the environment is becoming increasingly critical [1]. Organic dyes existing in industrial wastewater are one of the significant organic pollutants that have adverse effects on nature [2]. The existence of organic dyes in water can cause grave issues since a large number of them are potentially toxic and can be converted into life-threatening agents [3]. Today numerous dyes are used in industrial processes like cosmetics, printing, textiles, hair coloring, and leather tannery. Based on the chemical structure of the dyes, they can be classified into various groups, such as thiazine, anthraquinone, oxazine, methine, azine, xanthene, and azo [4,5]. Rhodamine B (RhB) is a part of the xanthene dye class and, thanks to its good solubility in water and excellent brightness, has been broadly utilized in the industry [6]. RhB is considered the most toxic xanthene dye, with a catastrophic impact on human health and the environment [7]. Therefore, removing dyes from wastewater is essential for environmental protection. Conventional pollution treatment technologies, such as adsorption, precipitation, membrane separation, and coagulation, need considerable investment and time [8]. Photocatalysis technology has been regarded as an effective wastewater treatment method, and due to the abundance and sustainability of solar energy, this method attracted much attention [9]. Photocatalysis can decompose harmful organic dyes under solar irradiation without producing secondary pollution [10].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous and crystalline materials constructed from metal ions and organic linkers [11]. MOFs' fascinating, countless, and tolerable structures make them highly promising for various applications, such as gas storage [12], heterogeneous catalysis [13], adsorption [14], and sensor [15]. MOFs are also expected to be good candidates for photocatalysis. So far, various MOFs have been used for photocatalysis, generally employed to degrade organic pollutants [16]. MIL-101(Cr) (MIL-Cr) is a commonly used MOF material due to its high surface area, good stability, and simplicity of usage. Nevertheless, the low catalytic efficacy of MIL-Cr is due to the deficiency of active sites, pushing researchers to design effective MOF-based heterostructures to remedy this issue [17]. For instance, Geng et al. showed that a unique NH2-MIL-101(Fe)/Cu2O nanocomposite exhibited a good photodegradation efficiency of 92% for RhB when exposed to visible light [18]. Amdeha and S. Mohamed employed ZnO/MIL-101(Fe) as a heterogeneous photocatalyst for RhB degradation that degrades 97.1% of RhB during 300 minvisible light irradiation [19].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained much interest in recent years as a new exciting nanomaterial in the carbon family with unique physicochemical features such as effective light-harvesting, upconversion luminescence (UCPL), and rapid electron transfer capability. CQDs can serve as an electron receptor, photosensitizer, or spectral converter, based on the photocatalytic system when used as a co-catalyst [20]. However, CQDs performance continues to fall short of expectations. Therefore, improving CQDs' efficiency and expanding their application range is a pressing issue. Doping CQDs with nonmetallic elements, like nitrogen, can improve their optical stability, quantum yield, and catalytic activity. Also, it has been demonstrated that N-doped CQDs can improve electron transferability and induce charge delocalization effectively. Therefore, nitrogen doping enhances the photocatalytic activity and results in exhibiting novel and interesting chemical and physical characteristics in CQDs [21]. Nugraha et al. used WO3/N-CQDs composite for the photodegradation of methylene blue. The WO3/N-CQDs composite exhibited lower band gap energy and better photocatalytic degradation performance than pristine WO3 [22].In another work, Zhao et al. prepared N-CQDs/InNbO4 composite to decompose ipronidazole under visible light irradiation. The results showed that Incorporating N-CQDs into InNbO4 could enhance its visible-light absorption and the composite had better photocatalytic performance than pure InNbO4 [23].

Thanks to their high specific surface area, MOFs are good candidates for supporting CQD nanomaterials in order to improve their photocatalytic efficiencies. Such anchored CQDs with good electro-conductivity and conjugated π structure can demonstrate excellent electron transfer/reservoir capabilities. Consequently, they can help efficiently separate photogenerated electron holes and significantly improve the photocatalytic efficiency of MOFs [24,25]. According to our knowledge, MIL-Cr/N-CQDs composite has never been used for photocatalytic applications. In this work, photostable and affordable N-CQDs were synthesized via the hydrothermal technique. Subsequently, some composites of MIL-Cr with different amounts of N-CQDs were prepared facilely at room temperature. The photo-degradation abilities of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) composites for RhB removal were investigated under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic ability of MIL-Cr loaded with N-CQDs significantly increased as a consequence of the findings. In addition, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite demonstrated good stability and reusability during three cycles.

1,4-Benzenedicarboxylic acid (BDC), chromium nitrate nonahydrate (Cr(NO3)3·9H2O), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), Hydrogen peroxide (30% w/w), Rhodamine B (RhB), carbon tetrachloride (CCl4), and ethanol (EtOH) were purchased from Aldrich Chemical Company. Acetone ((CH3)2CO), ethylenediamine (EDA), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and citric acid (CA) were provided from Alfa Aesar. All of the chemicals were commercially accessible and directly used as provided.

PW1730 X-ray diffractometer (XRD; PHILIPS Company, Netherland) was used to determine crystal structures of materials using Cu k radiation with a scanning range of 2θ = 1-10°. Using the KBr pellet method, FT-IR spectra were recorded using a Nicolet IR 100 (Thermo Nicolet, USA). Both a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM; JSM-6700F, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) were used to examine the morphology and structure of the produced materials (HRTEM; Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, Netherland). The nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms with surface area measurement were carried out using a BELSORP MINI II instrument from BEL Company with a BEL PREP VAC II degassing device. All of the samples were heated for 5 h at 100°Celsius before being analyzed to get rid of any unwanted molecules that had been absorbed. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) technique was used to measure the samples' surface areas. Using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2600, Shimadzu, Japan) and BaSO4 as a reference, we were able to capture the samples' UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), cyclic voltammetry, and Mott-Schottky analysis, were conducted on PGSTAT302N-High Performance at ambient temperature. Glassy carbon (2 mm in diameter) was used as the working electrode. At the same time, a platinum disk (also 2 mm in diameter) served as the counter electrode, SCE as the reference electrode, and 0.1 mol.L-1 Na2SO3 solution (volume of 20 mL) served as the electrolyte. The working electrode was modified in the following manner: To do this, we used an ultrasonic bath to disperse 3 mg of powder in 100 µl of ethanol for 5 min. 5 µl of the dispersed mixture was poured over the electrode and let to dry under IR light. After that, 2 µl of Nafion solution was applied and left to dry similarly. The Shimadzu UV-2450 (Japan) UV–Vis spectrometer was used to get the UV–Vis spectroscopic data. The EDAX Genesis 2000 Energy Dispersive Spectrometer was used for the EDX analysis. DLS analysis was carried out by HORIBA SZ-100 (Japan) with the 90° of scattering angle to find out the particle size of N-CQDs.

The MIL-Cr was obtained without using HF in accordance with the previous method [26]. 3.36 mmol of Cr(NO3)3·9H2O and 1.99 mmol of BDC were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water (DW) under magnetic stirring at ambient conditions. The mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated up to 210 °C at a rate of 1.5 °C/min, followed by constant heating at 210 °C for 8 h. The green product was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min and washed three times with DW. In order to get rid of the extra BDC ligands, the precipitate was heated in DMF for a full day while stirred constantly. Afterward, soxhlet extraction with ethanol was used to do the solvent exchange. Finally, the product was activated at 150 °C for 24 h.

In a typical one-step hydrothermal method [27], 1 g of CA along with 5 mL of EDA were added to 15 mL DW and kept stirring until a clear solution was achieved. Afterward, the solution was poured into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The final brownish solution was filtered with a 0.22-µM filter membrane three times, centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 45 min, and dialyzed against distilled water for purification; finally, a yellow solution of N-CQDs was obtained.

The photocatalytic activity of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) composites was conducted by utilizing four white LED lamps (Each lamp has a power of 15 W) as the visible light source. RhB was chosen as the model of aquatic pollutants. In a typical procedure, 20 mg of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(x) was added to 50 mL of RhB solution (20 ppm). Prior to irradiation, the mixture was stirred for 30 min in order to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, a little amount of H2O2)30% w/w) was added to the mixture and exposed to visible light irradiation. During the photoreaction, approximately 3 mL of the solution was withdrawn from the reaction every 20 min and submitted to centrifugation to separate the photocatalyst. To figure out the RhB concentration, the absorbance of RhB at λmax = 553 nm was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The effective synthesis of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) was affirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, O-C-O symmetrical and asymmetrical, C = C and C = O stretching vibrations could be responsible for the peaks at 1402,1621,1508, and 1700 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of MIL-Cr, respectively. Also, the peaks at 750, 883, 1018, and 1159 cm−1 are attributed to C-H deformation vibrations, which all confirm the presence of the benzene ring in MIL-Cr. In most cases, Oxygen-Metal stretching vibration can be seen in the range of 400–800 cm−1, and the peak at 674 cm−1 is attributed to Cr-O vibration. A possible explanation for the observed peak at 3400 cm−1 is the acidic O-H of carboxylic groups [28,29]. The FT-IR spectrum of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite exhibits two new peaks at 1052 and 1592 cm−1 owing to C-N stretching and N-H bending vibrations, respectively. Furthermore, the peak at 2927 cm−1 has a higher intensity ratio than MIL-Cr owing to the C-H vibration of N-CQDs, indicating that N-CQDs were effectively loaded on the surface of MIL-Cr [30].

The XRD patterns of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) are shown in Fig. 1b. The MIL-Cr framework is well-formed, as shown by the fact that the diffraction peaks of MIL-Cr are compatible with the simulated pattern acquired from CIF data. Since N-CQDs have amorphous nature, the intensity of the peaks in the XRD pattern decreases as the amount of N-CQDs increases on the surface of the MIL-Cr. MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(0.5), MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(1), and MIL-Cr closely match XRD patterns, suggesting that the crystalline phase of MIL-Cr has remained well. However, the peaks in MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) XRD pattern reduced in comparison with MIL-Cr, but the characteristic peaks are still observable. Nevertheless, the high amount of N-CQDs in the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(3) composite resulted in the disappearance of the peaks. This finding is consistent with the results of photocatalytic degradation efficiency in section 3.2, which MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(3) showed lower photo-dye degradation efficiency [31].

As seen in Fig 2 , N2 adsorption–desorption analysis was performed for MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs composite. Nitrogen gas sorption isotherm for MIL-Cr exhibits a Langmuir-type shape (type I), which has a sharp slope at low relative pressures (P/P0 < 0.2), and the slope decreases dramatically as the relative pressure increases, indicating the characteristics of microporous compounds. At high relative pressures (P/P0 = 0.8–0.99), hysteresis loop exists because of the interstitial voids formed by the small crystal particles. Type I isotherm is also observed for MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite, with a steep slope at lower pressure (P/P0 < 0.02) and a decline at higher pressure [32,33]. However, the nitrogen gas uptake is reduced because N-CQDs are located on the surface of MIL-Cr. Also, the BET surface area of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite (334.36 m2.g−1) is 5.53 times decreased compared with MIL-Cr (1851.5 m2.g−1). Therefore, the findings suggest that N-CQDs are loaded successfully on the surface of MIL-Cr.

EDX analysis and elemental mapping were also investigated for further proof of the excellent synthesis of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite (Fig.3 ). Elements of C, N, O, and Cr were identified in MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite structure using EDX spectroscopy, and their uniform distribution was confirmed by elemental mapping, which indicated that N-CQDs are well distributed in MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) framework.

According to the FE-SEM image shown in Fig. 4 a, MIL-Cr exhibits a regular octahedral structure with uniform sizes around 100–150 nm, which is in line with prior observations [34]. The FE-SEM image of the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite (Fig. 4b) reveals that the surface of the composite is much smoother than that of MIL-Cr, which may be attributed to the existence of N-CQDs on the MIL-Cr surface. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 4c, the TEM image of the composite confirmed that the octahedral shape of MOF was preserved after the addition of N-CQDs at ambient conditions, as previously suggested by the XRD technique. Also, the existence of N-CQDs was confirmed by HR-TEM analysis. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the dark-black spots of N-CQDs with an average size of about 3 nm can be detected on the surface of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite, which is in agreement with the findings of the DLS analysis of pure N-CQDs (the inset of Fig. 4d).

The optical characteristics of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite were studied by analyzing their DRS spectra. Fig. 5 a indicates that the absorbance peak at the wavelengths under 400 nm correlates to the π-π* transition in terephthalate ligand, whereas the peak observed in the visible region (400–700 nm) is assigned to the d-d transition of Cr+3 [35]. Band gap energies of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite were also determined by using the Tauc formula, (αhʋ)2 = B(hʋ-Eg), where absorption coefficient, photon energy, and energy of the band gap were respectively denoted by α, hʋ, and Eg. The energy of the band gap was calculated to be 2.58 eV for MIL-Cr, and 1.95 eV for MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) based on corresponding Tauc plots in Fig. 5b. Therefore, it is anticipated that MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite would have improved photocatalytic dye degradation efficiency due to their greater visible light absorption compared to MIL-Cr.

The conduction band (CB) potential of MIL-Cr was measured by Mott-Schottky (M−S) technique. Due to the positive slope seen in the Mott-Schottky plots (Fig. 6 a), it was determined that the MIL-Cr is an n-type semiconductor. MIL-Cr has a flat band potential of −0.705 V vs. SCE, and the flat band of n-type semiconductors is typically 0.1 V more positive than the CB potential [36]. For this reason, we anticipate a CB potential of −0.55 V vs. NHE for MIL-Cr. Accordingly, it was found that EVB could be equal to 2.03 V, based on the Eg = EVB-ECB formula. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots (Fig. 6b) demonstrate that N-CQDs/MIL-Cr has a lower capacitance than MIL-Cr, which proves that N-CQDs/MIL-Cr allows more effective photoinduced charge separation and prevents electron-hole recombination. To investigate the electrochemical surface area (ECSA) of the MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2), cyclic voltammetry of the materials (Figs.S1 and S2) was conducted with varying scan rates (ranging from 20 to 120 mV.S-1). The linear slope of the plot (Fig. 6c) was used to represent the electrochemical double-layer (Cdl), and Cdl is supposed to be linearly proportional to active sites [37,38]. The electrochemically active surface areas of MIL-Cr and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) were found to be 0.0067 and 0.0074 mF.cm −2, respectively. So, the composite has a higher electrochemically active surface area than pure MIL-Cr and is expected to show better photocatalytic activity, which is consistent with the results of the photocatalytic dye degradation in section 3.2.

RhB was tested as an organic dye for degradation in the presence of visible light and hydrogen peroxide over prepared materials. Fig.7 a demonstrates that 64% of RhB can be degraded by MIL-Cr in 160 min. By loading different amounts of N-CQDs on the surface of MIL-Cr, the photocatalytic activity increased. The highest photocatalytic activity was found for MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite with 95% RhB removal efficiency, while MIL-Cr/NCQDs(0.5), MIL-Cr/NCQDs(1) and MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(3) composites exhibited lower activity. This finding implies that N-CQDs can enhance photocatalytic activity; nevertheless, the excess of N-CQDs on the surface of MIL-Cr can block out the incoming light. The real reasons for the higher photocatalytic activity observed for composite can be explained from photo/electrochemical analyses. First, the DRS spectra (Fig.5a) showed increased visible light absorption for the composite. Also, the composite has a narrower band gap than pristine MIL-Cr, which can promote the electron transfer rate and the effective use of visible light. Second, the EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 6b) revealed that the composite has lower capacitance, which resulted in the efficient separation of electron-hole pair and preventing the recombination. Third, the values of Cdl (Fig. 6c) showed that the composite has a higher electrochemically active surface area, which promotes better photodegradation of dye molecules.

As shown in Fig.7b, a variety of systems have been assessed to check out the impact of some factors on RhB decomposition. According to the obtained results, only 22% of RhB was decomposed by combining visible light irradiation with H2O2 in the absence of the photocatalyst (H2O2 + Vis), likely owing to the weak conversion of H2O2 to •OH. Also, the system containing MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite and visible light without oxidant (MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + vis) showed no significant RhB degradation (about 26%).When N-CQDs were added to the system and exposed to an oxidant and visible light (N-CQDs + H2O2 + Vis), only 20% of the RhB was removed. Moreover, MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 system in the dark condition demonstrated very low RhB removal of about 28%. Therefore, the synergy between visible light, hydrogen peroxide, and composite is a key factor in achieving the highest RhB degradation for the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system. Also, neither MIL-Cr nor N-CQDs exhibited good photocatalytic activity, further confirming the efficient charge separation efficiency in the final composite. Besides, a higher or lower dose of H2O2 was introduced to the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system (Fig. 7c) to find the optimum amount of hydrogen peroxide. As was previously mentioned, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite lacks appropriate removal effectiveness in the absence of an oxidant. Degradation efficiency was enhanced by increasing the H2O2 concentration from 5 to 10 mM. It is clear that the removal effectiveness drops down dramatically at higher H2O2 concentrations (20 and 30 mM), most likely because of the interaction of excess amount of H2O2 with OH radicals to form H2O and O2 molecules [39]. Fig.7d exhibits the efficiency of RhB degradation by showing the steady decrease in the intensity of the RhB absorption peak at 554 nm across various time intervals for the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system.

RhB photodegradation of different photocatalytic systems were compared in more detail using the pseudo-first-order (ln(C/C0) = -k1t) and pseudo-second-order ((1/C)-(1/C0) = k2t) kinetic models, where C0, C, and k (k1 and k2) are the initial concentration, the concentration of RhB at specific time and kinetic constant, respectively [40]. Table 1 provides a summary of the numerical values of k (k1 and k2) and the R2 obtained from kinetic graphs depicted in Fig. 8 a and b. Based on the findings, the RhB photodegradation over time obeys the pseudo-first-order kinetic model with higher R2 values for all photocatalytic systems. The estimated rate constant of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system is 0.01597, which is 2.63, 8.63, 9.28, 9.5, and 11.24 times higher than MIL-Cr + H2O2 + Vis, MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + Vis, MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2, N-CQDs + H2O2 + Vis, and H2O2 + Vis systems, respectively.

In order to find out the photocatalytic mechanism of RhB degradation, the reactive species must be examined in the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) + H2O2 + Vis system. Reactive species, including •OH, h+, and e- can be quenched by applying isopropanol (IPA), Na2EDTA, and CCl4 scavengers. Fig. 9 a reveals that CCl4 does not affect the degrading performance. However, both IPA and Na2EDTA considerably decrease the degradation rate, which shows that •OH and h+ play a prominent role in this photocatalyst system. The hypothesized mechanism for the photocatalytic performance of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs is based on the results of the EIS, M−S, and Tauc plots. To effectively separate photogenerated electron holes, N-CQDs operate as electron reservoirs. When an electron is excited from the VB of MIL-Cr to the CB through visible light irradiation (Eq. (1), it can move to the N-CQDs, making the N-CQDs a helpful material for separating the resulting electrons and holes. The high specific surface area of MIL-Cr makes it a great support for dispersing N-CQDs and increasing contact interface. As a result, MIL-Cr and N-CQDs can transfer electrons more efficiently. In turn, the N-CQDs' electrons would react with H2O2 to generate •OH (Eq. (2). At the same time, MIL-Cr's remaining holes interact with OH– to produce •OH (Eq. (3), which these generated radicals would subsequently take part in photocatalytic processes (Eq. (4). As an additional reaction, the h+ can immediately react with RhB to generate CO2 and H2O (Eq. (5). However, the redox potential of •OH/H2O (2.27 V) is more positive than the VB potential of Cr-MIL (2.03 V), therefore, water molecules are not able to produce hydroxyl radicals directly [41].
(3) MIL-Cr/N-CQDs + hν → e- + h+
(4) H2O2 + e- → •OH + OH–
(5) OH– + h+ → •OH
(6)
•OH + RhB → CO2 + H2O
(7) h+ + RhB → CO2 + H2O

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploring the stability of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite
ce:section.ce:para: The MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) reusability was determined by applying the composite to three photocatalytic cycles of RhB degradation. To get rid of any leftover adsorbed RhB on the surface of the composite, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) were centrifuged and soxhlet washed with methanol for 24 h after each cycle and thermally activated prior to use. Fig. 9b demonstrates that around 75% of RhB is continuously removed within three photocatalytic cycles. The findings indicate the relatively high stability of MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) at applied photocatalytic reaction conditions.

Furthermore, the photodegradation of RhB under visible light was compared with other MOF-based composites. As can be seen in Table 2 , the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite is competitive and even more effective than other examples in RhB photodegradation. Also, the light source in the present work (LEDs lamps) is much more affordable than other works (Xe lamps), showing that the prepared MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite can be a good candidate for photocatalytic water remediation systems.

In summary, the MIL-Cr and N-CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal process. Since MIL-Cr is an excellent porous support for N-CQDs and prohibits the aggregation of N-CQDs, the composites with different amounts of N-CQDs were prepared by a simple solvent-deposition procedure. The results of the FE-SEM, FT-IR, XRD studies, and other physicochemical analyses, showed that the composite was synthesized successfully, and the crystal structure of MOF remained unchanged. Also, the existence of N-CQDs on the surface of MOF with the approximate size of 3 nm was confirmed by HRTEM analysis. According to the results of the photocatalytic experiments, the MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than MIL-Cr due to the electron-accepting capability of N-CQDs and hence prolonging the photo-excited state lifetime. Accordingly, both materials can help each other to demonstrate better photocatalytic activity and the dye degradation experiments in different systems confirmed the claim. The MIL-Cr/N-CQDs(2) composite had the highest photocatalytic removal efficiency among other composites, removing more than 95 percent of RhB dye in only 160 min while exposed to visible light. In the end, the scavenging tests depicted that •OH and h+ are responsible for dye degradation, and the photocatalyst was found reusable in successive photocatalytic runs.

Milad Rahmani: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – Original draft preparation, Visualization, Investigation, Validation. Alireza Abbasi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mahdiyeh-Sadat Hosseini: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from University of Tehran.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115019.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
